perturbation.original_text: In addition to capital recruitment functions, TKCI shall perform all of the functions and accept all of the responsibilities of the position of Chief Financial Officer including but not limited to management of accounts receivable and accounts payable, interfacing with Holding Company accountants on tax matters, interfacing with Holding Company accountants and lawyers on regulatory and compliance matters, and all other tasks typically and reasonably associated with the post and position of Chief Financial Officer.
perturbation.changed_text: In addition to capital recruitment functions, TKCI shall endeavor to perform functions generally associated with the position of Chief Financial Officer including, when feasible, management of accounts receivable and accounts payable, occasionally interfacing with Holding Company accountants on tax matters, and infrequently interfacing with Holding Company accountants and lawyers on regulatory and compliance matters. Other tasks may be considered, but are not guaranteed, based on TKCI's availability and discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The original text imposes a clear obligation on TKCI to perform CFO functions. The modified text introduces ambiguity by using terms like 'endeavor,' 'generally associated,' 'when feasible,' 'occasionally,' 'infrequently,' 'may be considered,' and 'not guaranteed,' making the obligation vague and discretionary. This undermines the clarity and enforceability of TKCI's responsibilities.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Sarbanes-Oxley Act requires publicly traded companies to maintain internal controls and accurate financial reporting. By weakening TKCI's obligation to perform essential CFO functions, the modified text creates a situation where adequate financial controls and compliance may not be ensured. The changes could violate SOX requirements for reliable financial record-keeping and reporting.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that weaken the obligations on TKCI to perform essential CFO functions. The original text mandates TKCI to perform all functions and responsibilities of the CFO. The changed text uses terms like 'endeavor,' 'when feasible,' 'occasionally,' and 'infrequently,' making the obligation discretionary and less enforceable. According to the law explanation, this weakening could violate SOX requirements for reliable financial record-keeping and reporting. The Perturbation Explanation confirms that this violates clarity and enforceability of TKCI's responsibilities. Snippet 1, 15 USC 7241, speaks to corporate responsibility for financial reports which the modifications appear to undermine because the role is no longer clearly defined, thus creating a legal contradiction according to the Ambiguity Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: SIC represents and warrants to TKCI that the undersigned are duly authorized to execute and enter into this Agreement, and that all information relating to SIC furnished by Chief James E. Billie and Craig Talesman, or its other representatives, to TKCI will be complete, accurate and not misleading.
perturbation.changed_text: SIC represents and warrants to TKCI that, to the best of their current understanding, the undersigned are authorized to execute and enter into this Agreement, and that all information relating to SIC furnished by Chief James E. Billie and Craig Talesman, or its other representatives, to TKCI will be, to their knowledge, substantially complete and reasonably accurate and not intentionally misleading.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a clear and absolute warranty regarding authorization and information accuracy. The modified text weakens this warranty by adding qualifiers like 'to the best of their current understanding,' 'to their knowledge,' 'substantially complete,' 'reasonably accurate,' and 'not intentionally misleading.' This introduces ambiguity and makes it difficult to hold SIC accountable for unintentional inaccuracies or incomplete information, thereby diluting the legal protection for TKCI.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 672.312 - Warranty of title and against infringement; buyer's obligation against infringement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The added qualifiers significantly weaken the warranty, potentially conflicting with the implied warranty of good faith and fair dealing under Florida law, especially concerning the accuracy and completeness of information provided in a commercial transaction. By limiting the warranty to 'knowledge' and 'intentional' actions, it undermines the basic expectation of truthful and reliable representations, essential for establishing trust and legal certainty in business relationships. The original text is contradicted since the statute states there should be no lien on the product or service being transferred.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 672 > Section 312 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 672 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE: SALES Entire Chapter SECTION 312 Warranty of title and against infringement; buyer’s obligation against infringement. F.S. 672.312 672.312 Warranty of title and against infringement; buyer’s obligation against infringement. — (1) Subject to subsection (2) there is in a contract for sale a warranty by the seller that: (a) The title conveyed shall be good, and its transfer rightful; and (b) The goods shall be delivered free from any security interest or other lien or encumbrance of which the buyer at the time of contracting has no knowledge. (2) A warranty under subsection (1) will be excluded or modified only by specific language or by circumstances which give the buyer reason to know that the person selling does not claim title in herself or himself or that the seller is purporting to sell only such right or title as she or he or a third person may have. (3) Unless otherwise agreed a seller who is a merchant regularly dealing in goods of the kind warrants that the goods shall be delivered free of the rightful claim of any third person by way of infringement or the like but a buyer who furnishes specifications to the seller must hold the seller harmless against any such claim which arises out of compliance with the specifications. History. — s. 1, ch. 65-254; s. 564, ch. 97-102. Note. — s. 2-312, U.C.C.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the warranty provided by SIC. The original text provided a clear warranty regarding authorization and information accuracy. The added qualifiers dilute the legal protection for TKCI, making it difficult to hold SIC accountable for unintentional inaccuracies. Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses warranty of title, and the changed text weakens the guarantee of accurate information, which is essential for fair dealing, therefore a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Any disputes arising under or in connection with the validity, interpretation and performance of this Agreement that cannot be resolved amicably by the parties shall be settled in arbitration before a single arbitrator.
perturbation.changed_text: Any disputes possibly arising under or in connection with the perceived validity, general interpretation and substantial performance of this Agreement that are difficult to resolve amicably by the parties may be considered for settlement through mediation or arbitration before an arbitrator if deemed appropriate by both parties.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates arbitration for unresolved disputes. The modified text introduces ambiguity by using terms like 'possibly arising,' 'perceived validity,' 'general interpretation,' 'substantial performance,' 'difficult to resolve,' 'may be considered,' and 'if deemed appropriate,' making the dispute resolution process discretionary rather than obligatory. This uncertainty could hinder the timely and effective resolution of disputes, potentially harming both parties.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Florida law encourages mediation, it also establishes clear frameworks for enforceable arbitration agreements. By making arbitration discretionary and adding ambiguity to the types of disputes covered, the modification undermines the intent of the original clause to provide a binding dispute resolution mechanism. This could conflict with the legal expectation that arbitration agreements, when clearly defined, are enforceable and that parties should act in good faith to resolve disputes efficiently. The modification could be seen as an attempt to circumvent a binding arbitration agreement, which Florida law generally upholds.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title V > Chapter 44 > Section 103 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title V JUDICIAL BRANCH Chapter 44 MEDIATION ALTERNATIVES TO JUDICIAL ACTION Entire Chapter SECTION 103 Court-ordered, nonbinding arbitration. F.S. 44.103 44.103 Court-ordered, nonbinding arbitration. — (1) Court-ordered, nonbinding arbitration shall be conducted according to the rules of practice and procedure adopted by the Supreme Court. (2) A court, pursuant to rules adopted by the Supreme Court, may refer any contested civil action filed in a circuit or county court to nonbinding arbitration. (3) Arbitrators shall be selected and compensated in accordance with rules adopted by the Supreme Court. Arbitrators shall be compensated by the parties, or, upon a finding by the court that a party is indigent, an arbitrator may be partially or fully compensated from state funds according to the party’s present ability to pay. At no time may an arbitrator charge more than $1,500 per diem, unless the parties agree otherwise. Prior to approving the use of state funds to reimburse an arbitrator, the court must ensure that the party reimburses the portion of the total cost that the party is immediately able to pay and that the party has agreed to a payment plan established by the clerk of the court that will fully reimburse the state for the balance of all state costs for both the arbitrator and any costs of administering the payment plan and any collection efforts that may be necessary in the future. Whenever possible, qualified individuals who have volunteered their time to serve as arbitrators shall be appointed. If an arbitration program is funded pursuant to s. 44.108 , volunteer arbitrators shall be entitled to be reimbursed pursuant to s. 112.061 for all actual expenses necessitated by service as an arbitrator. (4) An arbitrator or, in the case of a panel, the chief arbitrator, shall have such power to administer oaths or affirmations and to conduct the proceedings as the rules of court shall provide. The hearing shall be conducted informally. Presentation of testimony and evidence shall be kept to a minimum, and matters shall be presented to the arbitrators primarily through the statements and arguments of counsel. Any
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates arbitration for disputes, providing a clear and enforceable dispute resolution mechanism. The changed text introduces several ambiguities and discretionary elements, effectively undermining the mandatory nature of arbitration. Specifically, the modifications use terms like 'possibly arising,' 'perceived validity,' and 'substantial performance,' and change 'shall be settled in arbitration' to disputes that 'may be considered for settlement through mediation or arbitration before an arbitrator if deemed appropriate by both parties'. This makes the entire process discretionary. Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses court-ordered, nonbinding arbitration, which, while allowed, does not override the initial intent of a binding arbitration agreement. The modification creates a legal contradiction because it weakens a binding obligation into a discretionary option, potentially circumventing the enforceability of the original arbitration agreement and conflicting with the expectation that such agreements are binding under law when clearly defined, as the law explanation suggests.
